FT_IC1.evidence: Baseline data from 10,842 participants in the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, enrolled from 21 study sites between September 2016 and October 2018.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis using propensity-score matching of ABCD baseline data to compare screen time by autism status and assess associations with obesity.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Frontiers in Pediatrics (2023); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Table 2 reports ratio of means for 'Social and video chat minutes per day' separately (girls with autism 45.5 vs. 21.9 min, ratio=2.08, p=0.04); Table 3 reports prevalence ratio for texting and obesity (overall p=0.04, PR=1.42 for 30+ min). These are phone/SM-specific variables paired with autism status (neurodevelopmental diagnosis) and obesity (physical health outcome), both individual-trait-level.

FT_IC3.evidence: Prevalence ratios with 95% CIs reported in Tables 3–5; ratio of means with 95% CIs in Table 2; p-values throughout.

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple quantitative numeric results reported for SM/phone (social media/video chat, texting) paired with obesity and autism status outcomes.

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional (baseline data only); FT-EC7 requires a longitudinal design, which is not present here.
notes: Screen time categories are passive viewing (TV/movies/videos), video games, social media/video chat, and texting. Social media/video chat and texting are qualifying phone/SM variables. Obesity and autism status are qualifying individual-trait outcomes.

FT_IC1.evidence: Data from the ABCD Study, 8324 children aged 9-11 years from 21 U.S. sites, ABCD 2.0 release from NIH Data Archive.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: longitudinal resting-state fMRI study with baseline and 2-year follow-up, plus mediation analysis using ABCD data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Developmental Cognitive Neuroscience (2023); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific estimate found anywhere in main text, figures, or referenced supplementary tables. The sole exposure variable is the Screen-Activity Proportion (SAP = DailyScreenTime / [DailyScreenTime + DailyActivityTime]). Step 2: The daily screen time component of SAP is computed from the ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey 14-item questionnaire covering TV shows/movies, watching videos, and playing video games — a composite that bundles TV, videos, and gaming. No exclusively phone/SM breakout is reported anywhere. The composite includes non-phone/non-SM items (TV, video streaming, video games on unspecified platforms) with no separate phone/SM coefficient. NOT_MET under Step 2.

notes: The SAP score bundles all screen types (TV, videos, video games) without isolating phone/SM use. No sensitivity analysis or supplementary table provides a phone- or SM-specific estimate.

FT_IC1.evidence: Data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, N=11,633, recruited from 21 diverse U.S. sites, ABCD 4.0 release, baseline 2016-2018.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort study analyzing ABCD data with logistic regression of baseline screen time predicting 2-year follow-up suicidal behaviors.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Preventive Medicine (2023); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Table 2 reports separate adjusted ORs for texting (aOR 1.36, 95% CI 1.06-1.74, p=0.014) and video chat (aOR 1.30, 95% CI 1.03-1.65, p=0.028) as IVs predicting suicidal behaviors (KSADS-5 clinical outcome) at 2-year follow-up. Both texting and video chat are qualifying phone/SM-specific variables. Suicidal behaviors (psychiatric clinical diagnosis) is an individual-trait-level outcome.

FT_IC3.evidence: Adjusted ORs with 95% CIs and p-values reported in Table 2 for each screen time modality including texting, video chat, social networking, video games, videos, and total screen time.

FT_IC4.evidence: Logistic regression with adjusted ORs, 95% CIs, and p-values reported for all screen time modalities paired with suicidal behaviors outcome.

FT_EC7_reason: SM/phone variables (texting, video chat, social networking) serve as IV (exposure) predicting suicidal behaviors (DV/outcome). EC7 requires SM to be DV in every analysis with no SM-as-IV analysis anywhere; that condition is not met here.
notes: Paper reports separate modality-level aORs for texting (1.36), video chat (1.30), social networking (1.27, ns after adjustment), videos (1.21), and video games (1.18). Total screen time aOR=1.09. Social media/texting/video chat are qualifying phone/SM variables as IVs.
